author: mariusvanderwijden
content: I misunderstood the question here. I agree that the EL doesn't need the payloads from the cl. We will trigger a full sync in this case

author: lukaszrozmej
content: <@755590043632140352> IMO it is fine. From Nethermind perspective FCU is crucial to do a catch-up sync, NewPayload is not enough.

author: lukaszrozmej
content: and by catch-up I mean it will grab payloads from network and execute them

author: potuz
content: So no danger that we could trigger a snap sync?

author: mariusvanderwijden
content: <@402853454126776320> Once we finished a snap sync, we never go back to it no matter what, right? (i am pretty sure)

author: lukaszrozmej
content: <@755590043632140352> it would depend on implementation, but we agreed on Paris that we shouldn't do catch-up by syncing state but only by processing blocks. If someone is offline for months and wants to catch-up we recommend fresh sync

author: potuz
content: Thanks I'm starting to suspect that we can greatly simplify CL syncing without dropping the trust assumptions of today

author: nero_eth
content:
I think if the head block's state is missing (e.g. a reorg deeper than the state diffs geth keeps)  or if there's a reorg past the snap sync pivot block, then geth would fallback to snap-sync. 
Please double-check that though.

author: pawandhananjay
content:
how would snap sync work in the case of a fresh sync? i would assume the EL would only fetch the finalized state and then execute all blocks after the finalized block from the initial fcu? if we completely deprecate payload by range on the CL, my concern is this:
1. CL checkpoint syncs to a finalized state, sends a fcu to the EL
2. EL starts snap syncing
3. because we have no payload by range, CL goes into optimistic mode, catches up to head and sends another fcu.
4. at this point, does the EL still apply all the blocks between finalized and head or will the snap sync move to download the new head state without applying everything in between.

I'm guessing the EL applies all blocks, but i just wanted to confirm that is the case
